title: Find All Occurrences of a Pattern (KMP-style)
description: Given text and pattern, output all starting indices where pattern occurs.

examples.explanation:
Step 1: n=19, m=9, max_start=10
Step 2: i=0, compare text[0-8]='ABABDABAC' with 'ABABCABAB', mismatch at j=4 (D vs C), break
Step 3: i=1, compare text[1-9]='BABDABACD' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (B vs A), break
Step 4: i=2, compare text[2-10]='ABDABACDA' with pattern, mismatch at j=2 (D vs A), break
Step 5: i=3, compare text[3-11]='BDABACDAB' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (B vs A), break
Step 6: i=4, compare text[4-12]='DABACDABA' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (D vs A), break
Step 7: i=5, compare text[5-13]='ABACDABAB' with pattern, mismatch at j=4 (C vs C match, continue), j=5 (D vs A mismatch), break
Step 8: i=6, compare text[6-14]='BACDABABC' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (B vs A), break
Step 9: i=7, compare text[7-15]='ACDABABCA' with pattern, mismatch at j=1 (C vs B), break
Step 10: i=8, compare text[8-16]='CDABABCAB' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (C vs A), break
Step 11: i=9, compare text[9-17]='DABABCABA' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (D vs A), break
Step 12: i=10, compare text[10-18]='ABABCABAB' with pattern, all 9 characters match exactly, add 10 to result

Step 1: n=5, m=2, max_start=3
Step 2: i=0, compare text[0-1]='AA' with 'AA', match, add 0
Step 3: i=1, compare text[1-2]='AA' with 'AA', match, add 1
Step 4: i=2, compare text[2-3]='AA' with 'AA', match, add 2
Step 5: i=3, compare text[3-4]='AA' with 'AA', match, add 3

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Iterate through each possible starting index in the text and compare the pattern character by character. Use if the pattern is very short or for educational purposes to understand the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Pattern Matching
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find all starting indices where pattern occurs in text using brute force comparison.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the pattern matching problem by systematically checking every possible starting position in the text where the pattern could begin. The intuition is straightforward: if we slide the pattern along the text from left to right, at each position we can compare characters one by one. This works because we're exhaustively checking all possibilities - if the pattern exists anywhere in the text, we'll eventually slide to that position and find a match. The algorithm starts at index 0 of the text and continues to index n-m (where n is text length, m is pattern length). At each starting index i, we compare text[i+j] with pattern[j] for j from 0 to m-1. If all characters match, we record i as a match. If any character mismatches, we break early and move to the next starting position. This early breaking is crucial for efficiency even in a brute force approach. Time complexity is O(n*m) in worst case (when pattern almost matches at many positions) but can be better with early mismatches. Space complexity is O(1) excluding output storage. The connection to real applications includes simple text search in editors or basic DNA sequence matching where patterns are short. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty pattern (should match at all positions), 2) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries (should go to n-m inclusive), 3) Forgetting to handle case sensitivity, 4) Not optimizing with early break on mismatch. The real code pattern involves nested loops with careful boundary conditions.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=19, m=9, max_start=10
Step 2: i=0, compare text[0-8]='ABABDABAC' with 'ABABCABAB', mismatch at j=4 (D vs C), break
Step 3: i=1, compare text[1-9]='BABDABACD' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (B vs A), break
Step 4: i=2, compare text[2-10]='ABDABACDA' with pattern, mismatch at j=2 (D vs A), break
Step 5: i=3, compare text[3-11]='BDABACDAB' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (B vs A), break
Step 6: i=4, compare text[4-12]='DABACDABA' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (D vs A), break
Step 7: i=5, compare text[5-13]='ABACDABAB' with pattern, mismatch at j=4 (C vs C match, continue), j=5 (D vs A mismatch), break
Step 8: i=6, compare text[6-14]='BACDABABC' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (B vs A), break
Step 9: i=7, compare text[7-15]='ACDABABCA' with pattern, mismatch at j=1 (C vs B), break
Step 10: i=8, compare text[8-16]='CDABABCAB' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (C vs A), break
Step 11: i=9, compare text[9-17]='DABABCABA' with pattern, mismatch at j=0 (D vs A), break
Step 12: i=10, compare text[10-18]='ABABCABAB' with pattern, all 9 characters match exactly, add 10 to result

Step 1: n=5, m=2, max_start=3
Step 2: i=0, compare text[0-1]='AA' with 'AA', match, add 0
Step 3: i=1, compare text[1-2]='AA' with 'AA', match, add 1
Step 4: i=2, compare text[2-3]='AA' with 'AA', match, add 2
Step 5: i=3, compare text[3-4]='AA' with 'AA', match, add 3

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Character-by-Character Comparison at Fixed Position
ladders.desc: Implement the comparison function that checks if pattern matches text starting at a specific index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of brute force pattern matching - it answers the question: 'Does the pattern match the text starting at position i?' The intuition is simple: we need to verify that every character in the pattern aligns perfectly with the corresponding characters in the text. We do this by iterating through the pattern positions j from 0 to m-1 and comparing pattern[j] with text[i+j]. If all characters match, return True. If any character mismatches, we can return False immediately - this early termination is what makes brute force somewhat efficient despite its O(n*m) worst case. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Check if i+m exceeds text length (pattern would go beyond text), if so return False. 2) For j from 0 to m-1: compare text[i+j] with pattern[j]. 3) If any comparison fails, return False immediately. 4) If all comparisons pass, return True. Time complexity is O(m) for each call. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being called for each possible starting position i from 0 to n-m. Common mistakes: 1) Not checking bounds before accessing text[i+j], 2) Continuing comparison after finding mismatch (wasteful), 3) Using wrong comparison operator (== vs !=). The real code pattern is a simple loop with early return on mismatch.

examples.trace:
Step 1: m=2, check bounds: 2+2=4 < text length 5, OK
Step 2: j=0, compare text[2]='l' with pattern[0]='l', match
Step 3: j=1, compare text[3]='l' with pattern[1]='l', match
Step 4: All characters matched, return True

Step 1: m=4, check bounds: 3+4=7 < text length 9, OK
Step 2: j=0, compare text[3]='o' with pattern[0]='r', mismatch
Step 3: Immediate return False (don't check remaining characters)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sliding Window Comparison
ladders.desc: Implement the sliding mechanism that moves the comparison window across the text.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of brute force pattern matching is the sliding window technique. The intuition is that we need to check the pattern against every possible alignment in the text. Imagine the pattern as a stencil that we slide along the text from left to right. At each position, we check if the stencil matches the text beneath it. The algorithm maintains a 'window' - a contiguous substring of the text with the same length as the pattern. We start with the window at position 0 (text[0:m]), then slide it right by one position to get text[1:m+1], and so on until the window reaches the end of the text. The key insight is that we only need to slide until n-m because beyond that point, the pattern would extend past the text's end. For each window position i, we extract the substring text[i:i+m] and compare it with the pattern. In implementation, we don't actually extract substrings (which would be O(m) space), but compare character by character. Time complexity for the sliding is O(n-m+1) iterations, with each iteration potentially doing O(m) work. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by providing the framework that uses the comparison subroutine at each position. Common mistakes: 1) Sliding beyond n-m (off-by-one), 2) Not handling empty pattern case (window size 0), 3) Using inefficient substring extraction. The real code pattern is a single loop from i=0 to n-m inclusive.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=6, m=3, n-m=3
Step 2: i=0, window='ABC'
Step 3: i=1, window='BCD'
Step 4: i=2, window='CDE' (this will match)
Step 5: i=3, window='DEF'

Step 1: n=2, m=3, n-m=-1
Step 2: Since m > n, no windows to check (pattern longer than text)

ladders.title: Building Block — String Character Access and Comparison
ladders.desc: Work with individual characters in strings and compare them systematically.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental operation of accessing and comparing characters within strings. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we need to be able to examine each character individually to perform pattern matching. In most programming languages, strings support indexing (text[i] gives the i-th character) and have a length property. The algorithm involves: 1) Getting the length of text (n) and pattern (m), 2) Using a loop to iterate through indices, 3) Accessing characters at specific positions using index notation, 4) Comparing characters using equality operator. The key insight is that we can treat strings as arrays of characters for comparison purposes. Time complexity for accessing a character is typically O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the indices and comparison result. This feeds into the sliding window by providing the ability to compare text[i+j] with pattern[j] efficiently. Common mistakes: 1) Assuming 1-based indexing (most languages use 0-based), 2) Not checking string bounds before access (causing index errors), 3) Confusing character comparison with string comparison, 4) Not handling different character cases. The real code pattern involves basic loop control with index variables and character comparisons.

examples.input: Compare characters in 'hello' starting at index 1 with 'ell'
examples.trace:
Step 1: text='hello', pattern='ell', start=1
Step 2: j=0, text[1]='e' vs pattern[0]='e', match
Step 3: j=1, text[2]='l' vs pattern[1]='l', match
Step 4: j=2, text[3]='l' vs pattern[2]='l', match
Step 5: All 3 characters match

examples.input: Compare characters in 'world' starting at index 0 with 'word'
examples.trace:
Step 1: text='world', pattern='word', start=0
Step 2: j=0, text[0]='w' vs pattern[0]='w', match
Step 3: j=1, text[1]='o' vs pattern[1]='o', match
Step 4: j=2, text[2]='r' vs pattern[2]='r', match
Step 5: j=3, text[3]='l' vs pattern[3]='d', mismatch

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Looping Through Indices with Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Implement controlled iteration through array indices with proper boundary conditions.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves creating loops that iterate through valid indices of a sequence while respecting its boundaries. The intuition is that when working with strings or arrays, we need to access elements using indices, and these indices must be within the valid range [0, length-1]. Going outside this range causes errors. The algorithm requires: 1) Determining the length of the sequence, 2) Setting up a loop variable (typically i) that starts at 0, 3) Continuing while i is less than length (for forward iteration), 4) Incrementing i by 1 each iteration. For pattern matching, we often need nested loops or loops with more complex bounds (like i from 0 to n-m). The key insight is that loop conditions must be carefully formulated to include all necessary indices but exclude invalid ones. Time complexity depends on loop count. Space complexity is O(1) for loop variables. This feeds into character comparison by providing the framework to iterate through pattern positions. Common mistakes: 1) Using <= instead of < for exclusive upper bound, 2) Off-by-one errors in loop limits, 3) Modifying loop variable incorrectly inside loop, 4) Not handling empty sequences (length=0). The real code pattern is a standard for-loop or while-loop with careful condition.

examples.trace:
Step 1: length=5
Step 2: i=0, valid (0<5), access 'h'
Step 3: i=1, valid (1<5), access 'e'
Step 4: i=2, valid (2<5), access 'l'
Step 5: i=3, valid (3<5), access 'l'
Step 6: i=4, valid (4<5), access 'o'
Step 7: i=5, not valid (5<5 false), stop

examples.input: Iterate from 0 to n-m where n=10, m=4
examples.trace:
Step 1: n-m=6
Step 2: i=0 (0<=6), valid
Step 3: i=1 (1<=6), valid
Step 4: i=2 (2<=6), valid
Step 5: i=3 (3<=6), valid
Step 6: i=4 (4<=6), valid
Step 7: i=5 (5<=6), valid
Step 8: i=6 (6<=6), valid
Step 9: i=7 (7<=6 false), stop

testCases.input: n=10, m=2, iterate 0 to n-m step 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Character Sequences
ladders.desc: Learn basic string operations: length, indexing, and character access.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces strings as fundamental data structures consisting of sequences of characters. The intuition is that a string is like a train where each carriage is a character, and we can count the carriages (length) and look inside specific carriages (indexing). In programming, strings are typically immutable sequences where each character has a position starting from 0. Basic operations include: 1) Getting the length (number of characters), 2) Accessing a character at a specific index using bracket notation, 3) Understanding that indices range from 0 to length-1. For example, in string 'cat', length=3, index 0 is 'c', index 1 is 'a', index 2 is 't'. Trying to access index 3 would cause an error. The key insight is that strings enable us to work with text data at a granular level. Time complexity for length and access is typically O(1). Space complexity for these operations is O(1). This feeds into all higher levels by providing the basic understanding of how to manipulate text data. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Thinking indices start at 1, 2) Confusing string length with last index (length-1 is last index), 3) Trying to modify individual characters (strings are immutable in many languages), 4) Not accounting for spaces as characters. The real code pattern involves using len() function and [] operator.

examples.output: Length=5, characters: h(0), e(1), l(2), l(3), o(4)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Count characters: h,e,l,l,o = 5 characters
Step 2: Assign indices starting from 0: h=0, e=1, l=2, l=3, o=4
Step 3: Verify: 'hello'[0]='h', [1]='e', [2]='l', [3]='l', [4]='o'

Step 1: Single character 'a'
Step 2: Length=1
Step 3: Only index 0 exists, 'a'[0]='a'
Step 4: 'a'[1] would be invalid (index out of range)

approaches.name: KMP Algorithm O(n+m)
approaches.summary: Preprocess the pattern to build a longest prefix-suffix array (LPS) to avoid redundant comparisons when mismatches occur. Efficiently scan the text in linear time by skipping characters based on the LPS.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Pattern Occurrences Using KMP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of KMP algorithm to find all starting indices of pattern in text.
ladders.explanation: The KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) algorithm solves the pattern matching problem in O(n+m) time by preprocessing the pattern to create a Longest Prefix-Suffix (LPS) array. This array helps avoid redundant comparisons when mismatches occur during text scanning. The intuition is that when a mismatch happens, we don't need to backtrack in the text—instead, we use the LPS array to determine how many characters of the pattern we can skip while still maintaining potential matches. The algorithm consists of two phases: first, building the LPS array for the pattern (O(m) time), then scanning the text using this array (O(n) time). During text scanning, we maintain two pointers: i for the text and j for the pattern. When characters match, both pointers advance. When they don't match, instead of resetting j to 0, we use LPS[j-1] to determine the next j position. This allows us to skip re-examining characters in the text that we already know match parts of the pattern. The space complexity is O(m) for storing the LPS array. Common mistakes include incorrect LPS array construction (especially handling the case when j>0 after mismatch) and off-by-one errors in index management. The connection to L1 is that this full solution directly uses the LPS construction as its key subroutine.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build LPS for pattern 'ABABCABAB' → [0,0,1,2,0,1,2,3,4]
Step 2: Initialize i=0 (text index), j=0 (pattern index), result=[]
Step 3: i=0, j=0: text[0]='A', pattern[0]='A' → match → i=1, j=1
Step 4: i=1, j=1: 'B'='B' → match → i=2, j=2
Step 5: i=2, j=2: 'A'='A' → match → i=3, j=3
Step 6: i=3, j=3: 'B'='B' → match → i=4, j=4
Step 7: i=4, j=4: 'D'≠'C' → mismatch → j=LPS[3]=2
Step 8: i=4, j=2: 'D'≠'A' → mismatch → j=LPS[1]=0
Step 9: i=4, j=0: 'D'≠'A' → mismatch → i=5
Step 10: i=5, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=6, j=1
... continue scanning ...
Step 25: i=10, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=11, j=1
... continue until j=9 (pattern length) → found match at index 10 → add to result
Final: result=[10]

Step 1: Build LPS for 'AA' → [0,1]
Step 2: i=0, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=1, j=1
Step 3: i=1, j=1: 'A'='A' → match → j=pattern length → found at index 0 → result=[0]
Step 4: j=LPS[0]=0
Step 5: i=1, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=2, j=1
Step 6: i=2, j=1: 'A'='A' → match → found at index 1 → result=[0,1]
Step 7: j=LPS[0]=0
Step 8: i=2, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=3, j=1
Step 9: i=3, j=1: 'A'='A' → match → found at index 2 → result=[0,1,2]
Step 10: j=LPS[0]=0
Step 11: i=3, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=4, j=1
Step 12: i=4, j=1: 'A'='A' → match → found at index 3 → result=[0,1,2,3]
Step 13: j=LPS[0]=0
Step 14: i=4, j=0: end of text → stop

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Construct LPS Array for Pattern
ladders.desc: Build the Longest Prefix-Suffix array for a given pattern.
ladders.explanation: The LPS array is the core preprocessing step in KMP that enables efficient pattern matching. For each position i in the pattern, LPS[i] stores the length of the longest proper prefix that is also a suffix for the substring pattern[0..i]. Proper means the prefix/suffix cannot be the entire substring. The intuition is to identify how much of the pattern has already been matched when a mismatch occurs, allowing us to skip unnecessary comparisons. We build this array in O(m) time using two pointers: len (tracking the length of the current longest prefix-suffix) and i (current position in pattern). We initialize LPS[0]=0 since a single character has no proper prefix/suffix. For i from 1 to m-1: if pattern[i]==pattern[len], we increment len and set LPS[i]=len, then increment i. If they don't match and len>0, we set len=LPS[len-1] (this is the key insight—we use previously computed LPS values to avoid restarting from 0). If len==0, we set LPS[i]=0 and increment i. This dynamic programming approach ensures each character is processed at most twice. Space complexity is O(m). Common mistakes include incorrect handling of the len>0 case (must use LPS[len-1] not len-1) and off-by-one errors in array indices. This subroutine feeds directly into L0 as the preprocessing phase.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize LPS=[0]*9, len=0, i=1
Step 2: i=1, pattern[1]='B', pattern[0]='A' → mismatch, len=0 → LPS[1]=0, i=2
Step 3: i=2, pattern[2]='A', pattern[0]='A' → match → len=1, LPS[2]=1, i=3
Step 4: i=3, pattern[3]='B', pattern[1]='B' → match → len=2, LPS[3]=2, i=4
Step 5: i=4, pattern[4]='C', pattern[2]='A' → mismatch, len=2>0 → len=LPS[1]=0
Step 6: Still i=4, len=0, pattern[4]='C', pattern[0]='A' → mismatch → LPS[4]=0, i=5
Step 7: i=5, pattern[5]='A', pattern[0]='A' → match → len=1, LPS[5]=1, i=6
Step 8: i=6, pattern[6]='B', pattern[1]='B' → match → len=2, LPS[6]=2, i=7
Step 9: i=7, pattern[7]='A', pattern[2]='A' → match → len=3, LPS[7]=3, i=8
Step 10: i=8, pattern[8]='B', pattern[3]='B' → match → len=4, LPS[8]=4, done

Step 1: LPS=[0]*4, len=0, i=1
Step 2: i=1, pattern[1]='A', pattern[0]='A' → match → len=1, LPS[1]=1, i=2
Step 3: i=2, pattern[2]='A', pattern[1]='A' → match → len=2, LPS[2]=2, i=3
Step 4: i=3, pattern[3]='A', pattern[2]='A' → match → len=3, LPS[3]=3, done

ladders.title: Core Logic — Efficient Mismatch Handling Using Precomputed Information
ladders.desc: Use previously matched characters to skip redundant comparisons when a mismatch occurs.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of KMP is that when a mismatch happens during pattern matching, we don't need to backtrack in the text. Instead, we use precomputed information (the LPS array) to determine how many characters we can skip while preserving potential matches. Consider matching pattern P against text T. Suppose we've matched k characters of P (P[0..k-1]) with T[i-k..i-1], but P[k]≠T[i]. Naive approach would backtrack i to i-k+1 and restart matching from P[0]. KMP's insight: since P[0..k-1] matched T[i-k..i-1], and we know LPS[k-1] tells us the longest prefix of P[0..k-1] that's also a suffix, we can shift P so that this prefix aligns with the corresponding suffix in T. This means we can continue comparing from T[i] with P[LPS[k-1]], skipping re-examination of characters. The algorithm maintains two pointers: i for T (never decremented) and j for P. When mismatch occurs at j>0, set j=LPS[j-1]; if j=0, increment i. This ensures O(n) text scanning. The intuition is similar to how we use failure functions in finite automata. Time complexity for this scanning logic is O(n) when combined with LPS lookup. Space is O(1) beyond LPS storage. Common mistakes include decrementing i (should never happen) or incorrectly updating j. This logic is the heart of L1's text scanning phase.

examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=1, j=1
Step 2: i=1, j=1: 'B'='B' → match → i=2, j=2
Step 3: i=2, j=2: 'A'='A' → match → i=3, j=3
Step 4: i=3, j=3: 'B'='B' → match → i=4, j=4
Step 5: i=4, j=4: 'D'≠'C' → mismatch, j=4>0 → j=LPS[3]=2
Step 6: i=4, j=2: 'D'≠'A' → mismatch, j=2>0 → j=LPS[1]=0
Step 7: i=4, j=0: 'D'≠'A' → mismatch, j=0 → i=5
Step 8: i=5, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=6, j=1
... continue (pattern won't fully match)

Step 1: i=0, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=1, j=1
Step 2: i=1, j=1: 'B'='B' → match → i=2, j=2
Step 3: i=2, j=2: 'C'≠'C' → mismatch, j=2>0 → j=LPS[1]=0
Step 4: i=2, j=0: 'C'≠'A' → mismatch, j=0 → i=3
Step 5: i=3, j=0: 'A'='A' → match → i=4, j=1
Step 6: i=4, j=1: 'B'='B' → match → i=5, j=2
Step 7: i=5, j=2: 'C'='C' → match → i=6, j=3
Step 8: i=6, j=3: 'D'='D' → match → j=pattern length → found at index 3

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Longest Proper Prefix-Suffix for Substrings
ladders.desc: For a given string, find the longest prefix that is also a suffix (proper, not entire string).
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental to constructing the LPS array. For any string S of length L, we want to find the longest proper prefix that is also a suffix. Proper means the prefix cannot be the entire string S (so maximum length is L-1). For example, for S='ABABA', proper prefixes are '', 'A', 'AB', 'ABA', 'ABAB' and suffixes are '', 'A', 'BA', 'ABA', 'BABA'. The longest that appears in both sets is 'ABA' of length 3. The intuition is to identify how much of the beginning of the string repeats at the end, which helps in pattern matching because if those characters already matched, we don't need to recheck them. We can compute this by comparing prefixes and suffixes incrementally. A straightforward O(L²) approach would check all possible prefix lengths, but for LPS we need an efficient O(L) method using incremental computation. The algorithm uses two pointers i and j: i starts at 0 (prefix end), j starts at 1 (suffix start). While j<L, if S[i]==S[j], we found a matching pair, increment both. If not, and i>0, set i to previous match length using already computed values; if i=0, increment j. This builds the solution for all prefixes dynamically. Time complexity for computing for a single substring is O(L), but in LPS we compute for all prefixes simultaneously in O(m). Space is O(1) for computation. Common mistakes include considering the entire string as prefix/suffix (must be proper) or using inefficient brute force. This feeds into L2 as the basic operation needed to build LPS.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize i=0, j=1, L=5
Step 2: j=1: S[0]='A', S[1]='B' → mismatch, i=0 → j=2
Step 3: j=2: S[0]='A', S[2]='A' → match → i=1, j=3
Step 4: j=3: S[1]='B', S[3]='B' → match → i=2, j=4
Step 5: j=4: S[2]='A', S[4]='A' → match → i=3, j=5 (end)
Result: i=3, so longest proper prefix-suffix length is 3 ('ABA')

Step 1: i=0, j=1, L=4
Step 2: j=1: 'A'≠'B' → mismatch, i=0 → j=2
Step 3: j=2: 'A'≠'C' → mismatch, i=0 → j=3
Step 4: j=3: 'A'≠'D' → mismatch, i=0 → j=4 (end)
Result: i=0, so length is 0 (no proper prefix-suffix)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Prefix and Suffix of a String
ladders.desc: Check if a given prefix of a string matches a given suffix.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing two substrings of a string: one starting at the beginning (prefix) and one ending at the end (suffix). Given a string S of length L, a prefix length p (0≤p<L), and a suffix length s (0≤s<L), we check if the first p characters of S match the last s characters of S. For example, for S='hello' with p=2 and s=2, we compare 'he' (first 2 chars) with 'lo' (last 2 chars) – they don't match. The intuition is that in pattern matching, when we have a partial match, we want to know if part of the pattern's beginning aligns with its end, so we can skip re-comparisons. The algorithm is straightforward: extract prefix = S[0:p] and suffix = S[L-s:L], then compare character by character. Time complexity is O(min(p,s)) since we stop at first mismatch. Space is O(1) if comparing in-place without extracting substrings. In practice, for LPS construction, we compare incrementally without explicit extraction. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in substring indices or comparing improper lengths (e.g., p+s>L). This operation is the building block for L3's prefix-suffix computation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: S='ABABA', L=5, p=3, s=3
Step 2: Prefix: indices 0-2 → 'ABA'
Step 3: Suffix: indices 2-4 → 'ABA' (since L-s=2, L=5)
Step 4: Compare char by char: index 0='A' vs index 2='A' → match
Step 5: index 1='B' vs index 3='B' → match
Step 6: index 2='A' vs index 4='A' → match
Result: All match → True

Step 1: S='ABCD', L=4, p=2, s=2
Step 2: Prefix: 'AB'
Step 3: Suffix: 'CD' (indices 2-3)
Step 4: Compare: 'A'≠'C' → mismatch
Result: False

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Prefixes and Suffixes
ladders.desc: Understand what prefixes and suffixes of a string are.
ladders.explanation: A prefix of a string is any substring that starts at the beginning of the string. For a string S of length L, the prefixes are S[0:0] (empty string), S[0:1], S[0:2], ..., up to S[0:L] (the entire string). A suffix is any substring that ends at the end of the string: S[L:L] (empty), S[L-1:L], S[L-2:L], ..., up to S[0:L]. For example, for S='hello', prefixes are '', 'h', 'he', 'hel', 'hell', 'hello'; suffixes are '', 'o', 'lo', 'llo', 'ello', 'hello'. The intuition is that in text processing, we often compare parts of strings from the start or end, which is essential for pattern matching. A proper prefix is any prefix that is not the entire string (so length < L); similarly for proper suffix. This concept is foundational because KMP relies on comparing prefixes and suffixes of the pattern to avoid redundant work. Understanding this allows beginners to grasp why we care about prefixes matching suffixes – if part of the pattern repeats, we can skip re-checking those repeated characters. Time/space analysis is trivial: enumerating all prefixes or suffixes takes O(L²) time if done naively, but we only need specific ones in algorithms. Common mistakes include confusing prefixes with substrings (prefixes must start at index 0) or forgetting the empty string is a valid prefix/suffix. This is the prerequisite for L4's comparison operation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: String length L=2
Step 2: Prefixes: start at index 0, lengths 0 to 2:
  Length 0: S[0:0]=''
  Length 1: S[0:1]='A'
  Length 2: S[0:2]='AB'
Step 3: Suffixes: end at index 2, lengths 0 to 2:
  Length 0: S[2:2]=''
  Length 1: S[1:2]='B'
  Length 2: S[0:2]='AB'

Step 1: L=1
Step 2: Prefixes: lengths 0 and 1 → '' and 'A'
Step 3: Suffixes: same as prefixes for a single character
